html:
Borussia Dortmund (BVB.VI) - EUR 3.035

Borussia Dortmund is a globally known sports franchise priced like a structurally impaired microcap. At the Vienna last close of EUR 3.035 on May 15, 2026, the equity value is about EUR 335 million. That is roughly book value against EUR 326 million of IFRS equity at March 31, 2026, for a club that generated EUR 526 million of revenue in FY2024/25, controls one of Europe’s most valuable matchday platforms, and has a first-team squad Transfermarkt marks near EUR 498 million.

The stale bear case is that BVB is a football vanity asset: volatile UEFA revenue, thin margins, no control premium because of German governance, and a tendency to recycle player-sale profits into wages and expensive replacements. That critique is not wrong, but it is now backward-looking. The current equity price is anchoring on the bad part of FY2025/26, especially the Champions League playoff exit and the lowered full-year guidance to a EUR 12-22 million net loss. The market is underweighting what happened after that: Dortmund finished the Bundesliga season second with 73 points, its fifth-highest points total, and secured the 2026/27 Champions League base case with a 2-0 win at Bremen.

That changes next-cycle economics. FY2025/26 is a trough year because UEFA income rolled off early while the wage and amortization base remained built for European contention. FY2026/27 starts with Champions League league-phase revenue already de-risked. This is not a thesis that requires winning the Bundesliga or reaching a Champions League final. It requires Dortmund to monetize a normal top-four German club cycle: EUR 490-525 million of revenue, EUR 45-65 million of net transfer income, and EUR 110-130 million of EBITDA. Against an enterprise value around EUR 365-370 million, the stock is trading at roughly 3x next-cycle EBITDA and about 0.7x revenue.

The most important change is not a single player or one match. It is capital allocation. Hans-Joachim Watzke’s long management era has ended. Carsten Cramer is now management board spokesman after Watzke stepped down, while Thomas Tress remains finance head and Lars Ricken runs sport. The club then removed Sebastian Kehl and appointed Ole Book, whose reputation was built at Elversberg by finding underpriced players, developing talent, and creating squad value with constrained resources. That is exactly the skill BVB needs. Dortmund does not need to become Bayern. It needs to stop paying premium prices for low-resale squad filler and return to being Europe’s best listed player-development compounder.

The Q3 numbers already show why reported net income is the wrong anchor. In the first nine months of FY2025/26, revenue fell to EUR 371.4 million from EUR 393.3 million, but EBITDA rose to EUR 94.1 million from EUR 88.3 million, net transfer income rose to EUR 55.1 million, personnel expense was slightly lower, and advertising revenue still grew despite fewer Champions League bonuses, according to the Q3 report. The market sees a guided annual loss. The better read is that the commercial base is resilient, the balance sheet is fine, and one weak European knockout result masked a business that can generate serious gross cash flow when player trading works.

The asset backing is unusual for a EUR 335 million market cap. At March 31, 2026, BVB had EUR 615 million of assets, EUR 234 million of intangible assets largely tied to player registrations, EUR 200 million of property, plant and equipment, EUR 148 million of trade and financial receivables, EUR 9 million of cash, and only EUR 31 million of financial debt before leases. The public equity is pricing the club close to accounting equity while giving little credit for brand value, scarcity value, and the gap between carried player values and market values. Football assets are not liquid like inventory, but they are real. Dortmund has repeatedly converted them into cash through Dembele, Sancho, Haaland, Bellingham, Gittens, and others.

The useful social signal is not stock promotion. There is almost no meme-stock energy here. The fan conversation around Book and Cramer is about scouting breadth, avoiding expensive Premier League bench players, and rebuilding the talent pipeline. That matters because BVB’s biggest behavioral risk is fan and media pressure to “show ambition” through bad spending. Right now, the fan base is giving management some political cover to be more creative and more ruthless.

The summer 2026 transfer window is the first hard catalyst. If Book sells one mature asset well, adds two younger underpriced players, and avoids older high-wage comfort signings, the market will have to start valuing BVB as a EUR 500 million revenue club with recurring transfer economics rather than a trophy lottery ticket. The second catalyst is FY2026/27 guidance after a second-place Bundesliga finish. A clean guide back toward EUR 500 million revenue and EUR 110 million-plus EBITDA would make the current valuation look visibly wrong.

The downside is mostly sporting and behavioral, not solvency. Missing Champions League in a future season can remove EUR 50-80 million of high-margin revenue. Transfer mistakes can trap cash in wages and amortization. The 50+1 structure caps takeover speculation. The Vienna line is illiquid, and even the German line is small-cap by institutional standards. Those are real discounts. They do not justify pricing Dortmund at roughly 1x book and 3x normalized next-cycle EBITDA after Champions League qualification has already been secured.

The base case is not heroic: Dortmund stays a top-four Bundesliga club, reaches the Champions League league phase in most years, averages EUR 50 million of net transfer income, and keeps personnel expense broadly controlled around the current EUR 265-280 million band. That supports EUR 115 million of EBITDA and modest positive free cash flow through the cycle. A 4.5x EBITDA multiple still leaves a large football-governance discount and gives an equity value around EUR 500 million.

Bear target: EUR 2.20, down 28% from EUR 3.035. This assumes Book fails, the club misses a future Champions League cycle, player values roll over, and the stock trades at a distressed discount to book.

Base target: EUR 4.60, up 52%. This assumes normal 2026/27 Champions League economics, EUR 110-120 million EBITDA, disciplined transfers, and only a partial rerating to about 4.5x EBITDA.

Bull target: EUR 6.80, up 124%. This assumes Book restores the talent-arbitrage machine, one or two major sales reset cash flow, sponsorship and matchday revenue keep compounding, and the market values BVB closer to a scarce listed sports franchise than a chronically ignored German small cap.
